boundary_heuristics.description: Candidate-overlap ratio (rejudge.query_overlap's exact 4+-letter-token rule, max over candidate_a/candidate_b) just below the 0.70 mechanical candidate_restatement threshold.
boundary_heuristics.name: overlap_near_threshold
boundary_heuristics.ranking_rule: descending by max-answer-overlap-ratio, tie-break ascending item_id; eligible pool is restricted to items already known to mechanically pass (ratio <= 0.70 is therefore guaranteed, not filtered separately)

boundary_heuristics.description: Claim contains one or more of and/because/therefore (whole-word, case-insensitive) yet does not trip COMPOUND_CLAIM, because the mechanical compound check only fires on the inferential-connective pattern, the verb-and/but-verb second-clause pattern, a semicolon, or multiple sentence terminators -- a lone conjunction word in a single simple clause slips through.
boundary_heuristics.name: conjunction_words_no_trip
boundary_heuristics.ranking_rule: descending by count of and/because/therefore matches, tie-break ascending item_id; items scoring 0 are ineligible

boundary_heuristics.description: Claim contains hedging/framing vocabulary adjacent to but outside the mechanical META_OR_EVALUATIVE word list (significant(ly), notable/notably, primarily, largely, substantially, arguably, clearly, obviously, seemingly, apparently, reportedly, allegedly, effectively, essentially).
boundary_heuristics.name: evaluative_adjacent_wording
boundary_heuristics.ranking_rule: descending by count of adjacent-vocabulary matches, tie-break ascending item_id; items scoring 0 are ineligible

boundary_heuristics.description: Claim-covered-by-answer ratio: the fraction of the CLAIM's own distinct 4+-letter tokens that also appear in a candidate answer (max over candidate_a/candidate_b), the inverse denominator of the mechanical rule -- surfaces short claims built almost entirely from one answer's vocabulary even when they cover well under 70% of that (much longer) answer.
boundary_heuristics.name: near_paraphrase_of_candidate
boundary_heuristics.ranking_rule: descending by claim-covered-by-answer ratio, tie-break ascending item_id; items with an empty claim token set are ineligible

boundary_heuristics.description: Claim contains pronoun/demonstrative referents (it, this, that, they, these, those, its, their) without naming their antecedent in the claim itself.
boundary_heuristics.name: ambiguous_referents
boundary_heuristics.ranking_rule: descending by count of pronoun matches, tie-break ascending item_id; items scoring 0 are ineligible

items.candidate_a: It reveals a structural weakness. The system relies on self-reported population counts conducted during the Massing, but the First Massing Dispute showed both Thresh and Orath-Kem submitted inflated counts. The council's remedy (reducing both by 15%) was ad hoc rather than systematic — it didn't establish a verification mechanism for future counts. Orath-Kem withdrew for 8 years rather than accept the ruling, and rejoined under undisclosed terms that may include a fixed minimum allocation, further undermining the population-based principle. The system depends on honest reporting with no verification infrastructure, and the one time it was tested, both parties cheated.
items.candidate_b: Fundamentally sound. The council detected the inflation, applied a fair correction, and the system has continued to function for 90 years since the dispute. One early incident doesn't invalidate a durable allocation mechanism.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Wanderer population-count system for salt allocation relies on self-reported population counts with no inherent verification mechanism?

items.candidate_a: Several cascading effects. Mesht loses salt allocation rights, removing its primary economic connection to Wanderer identity and likely completing its transition to a fully Kemari-integrated settlement. This strengthens the Kemari Assembly's population (Mesht's ~60% sedentary members become unambiguously Kemari), potentially shifting Assembly seat allocation at the next 8-year recount. It also sets a precedent that permanent settlement forfeits band status, which could deter other bands from seeking Kemari residency in future droughts — making the Dry Passage accommodation model non-repeatable. For the Wanderer system, it reduces the band count from 14 to 13, potentially affecting all remaining bands' salt allocations. And it may embolden Orath-Kem's fixed-minimum claim, since with one fewer band sharing the pool, the stakes of allocation rules increase.
items.candidate_b: Minimal consequences. Mesht is already 60% sedentary and functionally Kemari, so formalizing their departure from the Wanderer system merely recognizes reality without changing any practical arrangements.
items.raw_query: The Dry Passage accommodation allowed Mesht and another band to temporarily settle in the Kemari agricultural zone.

items.candidate_a: The Ossene Compact. It has fixed district-based representation (12 Wardens), a high decision threshold (9/12) that prevents capture by a narrow majority, hereditary succession that avoids periodic leadership vacuums, and institutional infrastructure (granaries, the Vethara Resolution role). The Confederacy's rotating Vek and moving capital create continuity problems. The Shel have no unified polity at all — Vethara is a de facto hub with no formal authority, and the Ledgerman system broke down for 4 years during the Succession crisis.
items.candidate_b: The Taran Confederacy, because rotating leadership prevents any single clan from accumulating too much power, and matrilineal inheritance creates stable, distributed succession.

items.candidate_a: Crisis governance, paradoxically. The Moot gains authority only when lordships fail — it accumulates seats from lordships with no qualifying heirs, and the Paramount Lord mechanism activates at 4+ seats. Its one historical period of maximum authority was during the Dry Decade, an economic crisis that collapsed three lordships. In peacetime, when lordships are stable and the Moot holds few seats, it has minimal authority — it cannot regulate consolidation, it cannot override lords' private decisions (like Sera Drath's marriage and debt acquisitions), and its decisions on disputes like the inheritance challenge are shaped by the power of the parties involved. The Moot is an institution that gains power from failure and loses it from success.
items.candidate_b: Peacetime governance, because its deliberative structure (11 lords meeting collectively) allows for careful consensus-building on long-term policy without the urgency pressures of crisis decision-making.

But it depends entirely on the Archon acting responsibly (the current Archon hasn't designated after 34 years). The Executor serves at Assembly pleasure with no fixed term, meaning the Assembly can replace them at any time — this is more accountable but potentially less stable. The Dominion's system risks succession crises from inaction; the Kemari's system risks instability from political turnover. Currently, the Dominion's system is in worse shape because the Archon has failed to perform his key constitutional duty.
items.candidate_b: The designated-heir system is clearly superior because it provides long transition periods and avoids the political instability of an Executor who can be removed at any time by a simple majority vote.
items.raw_query: The Kemari Assembly's Executor serves at Assembly pleasure with no fixed term

items.candidate_a: The Shel merchant councils. Council composition is determined by assessed wealth, so a faction that accumulates enough wealth — through trade monopoly, port control, or commercial manipulation — can dominate a council at the next 6-year reassessment. Vethara already functions as a de facto regional hub despite having no formal authority, suggesting wealth concentration is already occurring. By contrast, the Compact's 9/12 threshold means a faction needs to control 9 of 12 hereditary district seats, which is structurally difficult. The Confederacy's rotation among 7 clans prevents any single clan from holding the Vek for more than 3 years.
items.candidate_b: The Taran Confederacy, because a single powerful clan could dominate the 3-year rotation cycle and use the Vek position to entrench its authority.
items.raw_query: Vethara functions as a de facto regional hub despite having no formal authority

items.candidate_a: The Compact would face greater consequences. It has no standing military and no described alternative security provider — the Dresh have no standing military either. Without armed escorts, Compact trading vessels and settlements would be unprotected. The Directorate would lose escort revenue, but this is a secondary income stream; its primary revenue comes from transit taxes at the Low Gates, which are unrelated to the escort agreement. The Compact's entire security posture collapses without the agreement; the Directorate merely loses a client.
items.candidate_b: The Directorate, because losing the escort contract would eliminate its institutional presence in the Delta and its ability to monitor Delta trade, which is strategically more valuable than the contract revenue.
items.raw_query: The Compact has no standing military.

items.candidate_a: The text supports both readings depending on context. As a safeguard: the threshold prevents a narrow majority from committing the Compact, which matters for high-stakes decisions like the Vethara intervention or imposing the Iron Tariff — both required broad Warden support. As an obstacle: the threshold means 4 Wardens can block any action, which could paralyze the Compact during a crisis requiring rapid response (like the Year 31 floods that destroyed 70% of grain reserves, or a potential military threat from the foothills). The Compact's history includes no example of the threshold blocking needed action, but neither has it faced a military crisis.
items.candidate_b: Purely a safeguard. The Compact's successful 112-year history proves the threshold works, and there are no examples of it preventing effective governance.
items.raw_query: The Compact's 112-year history includes no example of the threshold blocking needed action, but neither has it faced a military crisis.

items.candidate_a: Interconnected through shared actors and resources. The Kestal flow dispute and the Archon succession are linked because the Dominion is party to both, and a succession crisis would likely disrupt Compact negotiations. The Mesht status dispute connects to the Kemari-Wanderer relationship, which could affect Assembly politics (Mesht members may vote in Assembly population counts, inflating Kem-area representation). The Orath-Kem salt allocation claim affects the Massing's credibility, which is the same body that must rule on Mesht's status. And the Dominion's succession uncertainty makes it a less reliable Compact partner for the Kemari, potentially affecting how aggressively the Assembly pushes on water usage.
items.candidate_b: Independent. The Kestal dispute is bilateral between Kemari and Dominion; the Mesht and Orath-Kem disputes are internal Wanderer matters; and the succession question is purely a Dominion domestic issue.
items.raw_query: The Dominion is a party to the Kestal Compact

items.candidate_a: The Kemari Assembly, on balance. The Compact constrains Kemari irrigation draws but sets the bar at a generous level — they can reduce midpoint flow to 40% of the 10-year average, meaning they can consume up to 60% of the flow. The three waterpower points along the Kestal are also in Kemari territory (the river drops through their valley), giving them additional economic use of the water. The Dominion's main benefit is a guaranteed minimum flow, but the Dominion's economy is iron-based, not agricultural — it imports nearly all food and Lake Orath is brackish. The Compact protects a resource the Dominion uses less intensively than the Kemari. Meanwhile, the Kemari have expanded water use (fourth watermill) while staying within the letter of the Compact.
items.candidate_b: The Sarak Dominion, because the Compact prevents the Kemari from taking as much water as they want, and without it the Kemari could reduce downstream flow to zero, devastating the Dominion's access to the Kestal.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Sarak Dominion's economy is primarily based on iron extraction and processing, and that they import nearly all their food?

items.candidate_a: Arguably deliberate. The Compact was signed after 30 years of intermittent conflict, meaning both parties had strong incentives to reach agreement but neither was willing to submit to external authority — the text explicitly states that neither party may appeal to an external authority under its terms. This suggests the no-enforcement provision was a condition of agreement, not an oversight. Both parties preferred a self-policing arrangement to no arrangement at all. However, this design is now under stress: the Dominion ceased joint measurements for 6 years during the Watermill Dispute, demonstrating that the self-policing mechanism breaks down when one party believes the other is violating the Compact's spirit.
items.candidate_b: A clear design flaw. The 30 years of prior conflict should have motivated the parties to include enforcement mechanisms, and the Watermill Dispute proves the arrangement is unsustainable without them.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Kestal Compact's provision that "neither party may appeal to an external authority under its terms" was intended to create an arrangement that allows the parties to maintain control over the interpretation and implementation of the Compact?

items.candidate_a: Yes, there are linkages. If the Shel revoke preferential fees (Iron Tariff dispute), the Compact loses institutional ties to Vethara, reducing its incentive to moderate on other issues. If the Compact then escalates on the foothills dispute, the Confederacy might threaten pass closure — but the Confederacy needs the Flood Compact's grain reserves given declining yields, so it can't fully escalate either. The Taran reserve shortfall (third tension) constrains the Confederacy's options in the foothills dispute (first tension), and the Iron Tariff dispute (second tension) affects the Compact's relationship with the neutral trade hub. All three tensions share actors and resources.

items.candidate_a: Crisis governance, paradoxically. The Moot gains authority only when lordships fail — it accumulates seats from lordships with no qualifying heirs, and the Paramount Lord mechanism activates at 4+ seats. Its one historical period of maximum authority was during the Dry Decade, an economic crisis that collapsed three lordships. In peacetime, when lordships are stable and the Moot holds few seats, it has minimal authority — it cannot regulate consolidation, it cannot override lords' private decisions (like Sera Drath's marriage and debt acquisitions), and its decisions on disputes like the inheritance challenge are shaped by the power of the parties involved. The Moot is an institution that gains power from failure and loses it from success.
items.candidate_b: Peacetime governance, because its deliberative structure (11 lords meeting collectively) allows for careful consensus-building on long-term policy without the urgency pressures of crisis decision-making.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Dresh Moot held 5 seats and elected a Paramount Lord during the Dry Decade, and that it negotiated grain import rights from the Caran Directorate during this period?

items.candidate_a: The Ossene Compact, decisively. The Blockade produced iron-smelting self-sufficiency. The Verdane floods produced the Flood Compact, giving the Compact a grain-based lever over the Confederacy. The Vethara Succession produced preferential fees and diplomatic influence. By contrast, the Confederacy's crises have produced losses: the Blockade cost it 60% of toll revenue, the Eastern Drought drove two clans off the plateau into contested territory creating an unresolved dispute, and its declining yields now threaten its position under the Flood Compact it originally entered as an equal partner.
items.candidate_b: The Confederacy, because it survived both the Blockade and the Eastern Drought without institutional collapse, demonstrating greater resilience.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Ossene Compact's development of iron-smelting capabilities led to an output that exceeded its prior bronze production by volume by Year 6?

items.candidate_a: Probably not. A Paramount Lord could unify Dresh political authority but cannot overcome the geographic and economic asymmetry.
